skill.name: dependency-sync
skill.description: Detect new imports in changed code, map them to package names, and update dependency manifests with the right package manager.
skill.summary: Keeps dependency manifests aligned with imports added during code changes.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, script, network, filesystem, and environment patterns, but most are markdown cookbook examples or exclusion lists rather than executable code. The confirmed risk is that the skill intentionally runs package-manager commands, contacts registries, and updates manifests, so it is publishable with a medium supply-chain warning.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Dependency Installation and Manifest Mutation
medium_findings.description: The skill defaults to automatic installation and tells the agent to install missing dependencies. This can add unreviewed packages from registries and modify dependency manifests or lockfiles.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly enables automatic dependency installation and shows package-manager commands. The behavior is intended, but it creates a clear supply-chain and workspace modification risk.

medium_findings.title: Import-Derived Package Names Can Cause Supply-Chain Confusion
medium_findings.description: The cookbook examples fall back to package names derived from imports when no mapping exists. A malicious or mistaken import name could lead an agent to install an unintended or typosquatted package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fallback behavior is documented directly and is common in dependency tools. The risk depends on project context and package review, so medium severity is appropriate.

low_findings.title: Static Code Execution Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The child_process and worker_threads terms appear in a Node built-in exclusion list, and the TypeScript snippets are cookbook examples. I did not find evidence of executable JavaScript in the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged strings are inside markdown documentation and fenced examples. The surrounding text instructs agents not to install built-ins, which reduces rather than increases execution risk.

low_findings.title: Environment and Archive Library Alerts Are Mapping False Positives
low_findings.description: The dotenv, tempfile, zipfile, tarfile, and related names are listed as import mappings or standard-library exclusions. I did not find evidence that the skill reads environment variables or archives files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to markdown lists of module names. There is no executable code path showing credential access, temporary file creation, or archive processing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to run uv, npm, cargo, go, pip, poetry, yarn, pnpm, bun, and flutter package commands. These commands can execute installer lifecycle behavior and download code from registries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly listed as the intended workflow. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because dependency installation is the purpose of the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Default Auto-Install Without Prompt
dangerous_patterns.description: AUTO_INSTALL is true and PROMPT_BEFORE_INSTALL is false by default. This can allow package installation before a human reviews the dependency choice.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The defaults are clearly stated in the skill variables table. The risk is mitigable by changing configuration or adding human review.

content.value_statement: New imports often fail builds when package manifests are not updated. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to detect missing dependencies and install them with the correct package manager.
actual_capabilities: Identifies modified Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, and Rust files from git diff or task context.
Extracts common import and require patterns for supported languages.
Maps common Python and Node imports to package names using cookbook tables.
Detects package managers from manifests such as package.json, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml, and go.mod.
Classifies dependencies as production or development based on package type and file location.
Runs package-manager install commands and can trigger a documentation audit after additions.
limitations: This is an agent workflow, not a standalone dependency parser or installer.
Automatic installs can change manifests and lockfiles and may require network access.
Import-to-package mappings are incomplete for private packages and uncommon ecosystems.
Human review is recommended before installing packages from untrusted or unfamiliar registries.

use_cases.title: Update manifests after implementation
use_cases.description: Detect imports added during a feature change and add missing packages to the correct manifest.

use_cases.title: Add test dependencies correctly
use_cases.description: Classify test frameworks, type packages, and linters as development dependencies instead of runtime dependencies.

use_cases.title: Standardize dependency hygiene
use_cases.description: Apply the same dependency detection and install flow across Python, Node, Rust, Go, and Flutter projects.

prompt_templates.title: Check Changed Files
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow after a small code change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run dependency-sync on the files changed in this task. Report any missing dependencies before installing them.

prompt_templates.title: Install Missing Imports
prompt_templates.scenario: Standard workflow when package updates are expected.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use dependency-sync to find new imports, detect the package manager, install missing packages, and summarize the manifest changes.

prompt_templates.title: Separate Dev Dependencies
prompt_templates.scenario: Test or tooling changes where dependency type matters.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run dependency-sync with attention to test files and tooling imports. Put test, lint, build, and type packages in development dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Multi-Language Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review all modified Python, Node, Go, and Rust files. Map imports to packages, verify registry names, install missing dependencies, and explain any uncertain mappings.

output_examples.input: A FastAPI route imports asyncpg, and pyproject.toml does not list it.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies asyncpg as a production dependency, selects uv from pyproject.toml, and reports the package added to the project dependencies.

output_examples.input: A new Vitest test imports @testing-library/react in a package.json project.
output_examples.output: The skill classifies @testing-library/react as a development dependency and reports the package-manager command used to update the manifest.

output_examples.input: A changed file imports only Node built-in modules such as fs and path.
output_examples.output: The skill excludes built-in modules and reports that no package installation is required.

best_practices: Verify each import is used before installing a package.
Review package names for typosquatting risk before enabling automatic installation.
Run tests or a build after manifest and lockfile updates.
anti_patterns: Installing packages only because a string looks like an import.
Adding test frameworks to production dependencies without checking file context.
Skipping manifest detection and assuming one package manager for every repository.

faq.question: Does this skill install packages automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. The default workflow allows automatic installation, so teams should enable review for sensitive repositories.

faq.question: Which ecosystems are covered?
faq.answer: The skill documents workflows for Python, Node.js, Rust, Go, and Flutter package managers.

faq.question: Can it handle development dependencies?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes rules for test frameworks, type definitions, linters, formatters, and build tools.

faq.question: Does it detect standard library imports?
faq.answer: Yes. The cookbooks list Python standard library modules and Node built-ins that should not be installed.

faq.question: Is it safe for private registries?
faq.answer: It can be used with private registries, but package names and registry configuration should be reviewed first.

faq.question: Does it update documentation?
faq.answer: It can trigger a docs audit after adding dependencies when that integration is available.
